---
title: Quick Start
---

The IT for Research Team will set up the server(s) with the OS of your choice (Windows or Linux) and will set up a root account for you:

## Access to the server
### Linux servers
For access to a Linux server you will need to provide a personal SSH key protected with a passphrase. You will be able to access SSH from home.

::: {.callout-tip}
To create a new SSH key pair do the following:

```sh
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "my.name@vu.nl" 
```
Works on Linux, Mac and Windows command line. Make sure to enter a passphrase for better security

You keep the private key yourself, store it in `/home/<username>/.ssh/` (in Windows `%USERPROFILE%/.ssh/`). The ITvO team will ask for your Public Key.
:::

To access use the command:
```sh
ssh -i <mykey> <mymachine>.labs.vu.nl
```

::: {.callout-tip}
In Windows 11 a SSH client is installed by default and SSH commands work in the Command Prompt and Powershell, it is no longer necessary to install software such as Putty.
:::

### File transfer
You can transfer files from the command line to your server by using the [`scp`-command](https://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/xenial/man1/scp.1.html). Or use a GUI client such as [WinSCP](https://winscp.net) or [Cyberduck](https://cyberduck.io/). 

<!-- Link to a nice tutorial for beginning Linux users -->

### Windows servers
For access to Windows servers use Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP), only via 🔒[eduVPN institute access](https://vu.eduvpn.nl).

### Installing software
Administrator/Root access to the server allows you to install all the software you need, on Linux use the `sudo` command. 

::: {.callout-important}
Note that [IT for Research](/topics/itvo.qmd) can help you to get started and will automatically install OS security updates, but you are responsible for installing and maintaining software yourself!
:::

## Backups 
It is possible to take _snapshots_ or _clones_ of the virtual server yourself. This allows you to go back to a previously created configuration, you can do this before you make major changes to your application, for example. 

In addition, ITvO makes daily backups with which a virtual machine can be restored for up to 30 days on request.

### Creating a snapshot
Log in to the [OpenNebula web interface](https://one.labs.vu.nl), with your VUnetId as username.

::: {.callout-important}
To access the web interface from home use [EduVPN institute access](https://vu.eduvpn.nl).
:::

- Go to Instances > VMs and double-click your VM.

- Open the Storage Tab and locate your disk.

![Snapshot create](/public/tools/scicloud/snapshot.png)
- Click the camera icon to open a dialog.

![Snapshot dialog](/public/tools/scicloud/snapshot-dialog.png)
- Enter a name for the snapshor and click the "Take Snapshot" button.

### Restoring a snapshot
![Power Off](/public/tools/scicloud/poweroff.png)
- To restore a snapshot the VM has to be shut down. Click the Power button and select the Power Off button, the server will be send a shutdown signal.

- If snapshots are available downward chevron will be shown next to the disk, click on it to view the available snapshots.

![Revert](/public/tools/scicloud/snapshot-restore.png)
- Check the snapshot you like to restore and click "Revert".

![Power Off](/public/tools/scicloud/poweron.png)
- Power on the server by clicking the Play button

## Web applications
This guide assumes you are running Linux, unless your particular application does not support it we strongly recommend running web applications on a Linux server, especially those based on Open Source technology.

### Domain name
[ITvO](/topics/itvo.qmd) can easily configure a domain inside `.labs.vu.nl` for your server. It is also possible to use other custom domains, contact [ITvO](/topics/itvo.qmd) for advise. 

### HTTPS
A web application exposed to the internet should only be accessible by HTTPS. [ITvO](/topics/itvo.qmd) can also configure automatically renewing [Let's Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/) certificates for servers in the `.labs.vu.nl` domain. 

### Security

### Docker

## Web proxy
The Web proxy is not actively monitored, but is used to quickly shutdown abusive services.

##
